pattern: With the array a hundred thousand elements long, the same prefix-maximum / suffix-minimum decomposition still answers the instability score at every index in one linear sweep, so no per-index recomputation is needed even at this scale.
hints: Recomputing the max of everything before i and the min of everything from i onward separately for every i is quadratic and will not scale to a hundred thousand elements; both quantities can be carried forward instead.
Build a prefix-maximum array left to right and a suffix-minimum array right to left, each in one pass, then the score at index i is prefix_max[i] - suffix_min[i].
The first index (scanning from 0) whose precomputed score is at most k is the answer; if the scan finishes with none qualifying, return -1.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, first stable index is at position 3
statement example, no index is ever stable
single-element array, k as large as possible
strictly increasing array, index 0 is always stable regardless of k
strictly decreasing array: the running suffix minimum is the same final element at every index, so the score is flat and the answer hinges entirely on k
all values equal, so every index is stable and the first one wins
k exactly meets the minimum achievable instability score, off-by-one on the boundary
the global minimum sits in the middle, so the suffix minimum drops early and only later indices can recover a small enough score

stress.description: random arrays up to the full hundred-thousand-element constraint with random k
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    nums = [rng.randint(0, 1000000000) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(0, 1000000000)
    return [nums, k]

stress.description: strictly decreasing long arrays, the hardest case for the prefix/suffix scan
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    start = rng.randint(500000000, 1000000000)
    nums = [max(0, start - i) for i in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(0, 1000000000)
    return [nums, k]
